```
1. Decentralized Identity Systems Concepts
2. 
3. Today, most digital identity systems are built around a central identity provider. That provider signs users in, stores key identity data, and often sits in the middle of every trust relationship between people, applications, and organizations.
4. 
5. This model works, but it also creates several growing problems:
6. 1. Extensive personal data is collected and retained by identity providers (IdPs).
7. 2. Many organizations need custom trust relationships with each IdP through federation or other explicit trust arrangements.
8. 3. The centralized architecture of each IdP can become a single point of failure.
9. 
10. This article explains:
11. 1. Why decentralized identity is a compelling alternative to the current model.
12. 2. How verifiable credentials improve the way identity can be shared and verified.
13. 3. Why Microsoft Entra Verified ID is an important example of this new approach.
14. 
15. The problems of the current model
16. 
17. In the traditional model, identity is not something people truly own. A user signs in through an identity provider, and that provider controls the credential lifecycle, the sign-in process, and often the way identity data is shared with applications and partner organizations.
18. 
19. This creates four practical problems:
20. • Too much concentration of trust. When one identity provider or its surrounding infrastructure fails, many connected services can be affected at the same time.
21. • Too much stored data. Central systems often collect and retain more personal data than a verifier needs for a specific decision.
22. • Too much integration work. Organizations often need federation, guest accounts, or other explicit trust arrangements to work across company boundaries.
23. • Too little user control. Users usually have little visibility into who can access their data and how often it is reused.
24. 
25. A good way to summarize the issue is this:
26. The current model is excellent at account-based access, but less effective at enabling portable, privacy-friendly proof. It can say, “this person can sign in here,” but it is not as good at saying, “this person can prove one fact, and nothing more.”
27. 
28. What decentralized identity changes
29. 
30. Decentralized identity changes the center of the system. Instead of a central identity provider being involved every time, the user can hold digitally signed credentials in a wallet app on their own device.
31. 
32. These credentials are called verifiable credentials.
33. 
34. An issuer, such as an employer, university, or government office, signs claims about a person or organization, usually after verifying them through official processes and, in some cases, in-person checks. The holder stores that credential in a wallet, and a verifier can later check that the information is authentic and still valid through a trusted distributed framework, without needing to contact the issuer directly every time.
35. 
36. This model is often described using three roles:
37. • Issuer — the organization that creates and signs the credential.
38. • Holder — the person or entity that stores it in a wallet and chooses when to present it.
39. • Verifier — the organization that checks the credential before granting access, trust, or a service.
40. 
41. That shift is important because it turns identity from a stream of repeated account lookups into a system of reusable, trusted proofs.
42. 
43. A simple real-world analogy
44. 
45. A driving licence is a useful analogy. A government authority first checks a person’s identity and eligibility, then issues a document that states certain claims, such as the right to drive a certain class of vehicle, as well as age and other relevant information about the holder.
46. 
47. Later, the licence holder can show that document to another party, such as a police officer or a car rental company. That third party does not need to call the original authority each time to understand what the document means; it only needs to trust the issuer and verify that the document is genuine and belongs to the person presenting it.
48. 
49. A verifiable credential follows the same broad idea in digital form. The document becomes a cryptographically signed digital credential, the user stores it in a wallet, and the verifier checks the credential instead of collecting and storing large amounts of raw identity data again.
50. 
51. In this model, neither the verifier nor, in some cases, the issuer needs to retain all of the user’s information. Instead, the information is packaged in a verifiable digital form, signed by the issuer, and stored in the holder’s wallet.
52. 
53. Why this is better for users
54. 
55. The biggest improvement for users is control. A user can keep credentials in a wallet and present them when needed, instead of repeatedly filling in the same forms and sending the same documents to many different services.
56. 
57. This also supports data minimization. In many cases, the verifier does not need a full identity profile. It may only need proof of employment, proof of student status, proof of age, or proof that a person has passed an identity check.
58. 
59. That means the user can share less while still proving enough. This is a better fit for privacy, and it reduces the spread of personal data across many disconnected systems.
60. 
61. Why this is better for organizations
62. 
63. For organizations, decentralized identity is not just a user-experience improvement. It can also reduce operational friction and lower security risk and liability.
64. 
65. When an organization verifies a signed credential instead of storing every identity detail itself, it can reduce the amount of sensitive data it needs to protect. This can simplify onboarding, partner access, and other trust-based workflows.
66. 
67. There are several clear benefits:
68. • Less repeated identity proofing. A trusted issuer can verify a person once and issue a reusable credential.
69. • Lower data exposure. The verifier can ask for the proof it needs instead of collecting a full identity profile.
70. • Cleaner cross-company trust. Portable credentials can reduce the need to expand trust boundaries through federation for every scenario.
71. • Better fraud resistance. Credentials are signed and checked cryptographically, which makes tampering harder and verification stronger.
72. • Lower risk and liability. Credentials, once issued and verified, do not necessarily need to be stored by the organizations that issue or consume them. This reduces the amount of sensitive data they must retain and can make them less attractive targets for malicious actors.
73. 
74. This is why decentralized identity is often best understood as an addition to existing identity systems, rather than always as a full replacement. Centralized identity still works well inside a company’s own trust boundary, while verifiable credentials are especially useful when trust needs to move across boundaries.
75. 
76. Technologies and implementations such as Microsoft Entra Verified ID make this transition possible for organizations that want to adopt this model.
77. 
78. The role of the wallet
79. 
80. The wallet is central to the user experience. In Microsoft’s documentation, Microsoft Authenticator is the wallet app that can:
81. • create decentralized identifiers
82. • receive issuance requests
83. • store credentials
84. • respond to presentation requests
85. 
86. That matters because the wallet is where the user’s control becomes real. The user can review a request, consent to share a credential, and keep a record of where that credential was presented.
87. 
88. This is a major conceptual improvement over systems where identity is silently copied between services or reused with little visibility for the user.
89. 
90. Examples that make the value clear
91. 
92. The easiest way to understand decentralized identity is through real-world scenarios.
93. 
94. Employee proof
95. 
96. A company can issue an employee credential to a worker. That worker can later present it to a partner organization to prove employment status without needing a custom federation link between the two companies.
97. 
98. Microsoft’s Woodgrove and Proseware example shows exactly this pattern: one company issues proof of employment, and another company accepts that proof to grant a discount or service.
99. 
100. Remote onboarding
101. 
102. A verified identity credential can help with remote hiring and onboarding. After an identity proofing step, the new employee can use the credential to receive initial access or prove identity in later onboarding steps.
103. 
104. This can reduce manual review and avoid unnecessary copying of sensitive identity data into each internal system.
105. 
106. Access to external services
107. 
108. A person may need to prove something outside their employer’s trust boundary, for example employee status, supplier status, or membership in a program. A portable credential is often a better fit than creating yet another external account.
109. 
110. This is useful because the verifier can trust the issuer’s signed credential without needing the issuer to sit in the middle of every transaction.
111. 
112. Public and regulated services
113. 
114. The same pattern can apply to health cards, permits, student credentials, age checks, or local government services. The verifier only needs enough proof for the decision at hand, not a full copy of the user’s identity records.
115. 
116. That makes the model easier to explain to readers because it mirrors how official documents are already used in the physical world.
117. 
118. Where Microsoft Entra Verified ID fits
119. 
120. Microsoft Entra Verified ID is Microsoft’s managed service for issuing and verifying verifiable credentials. It is based on decentralized identity concepts and open standards such as W3C Verifiable Credentials and decentralized identifiers.
121. 
122. For a Microsoft-focused audience, the key point is that Verified ID extends identity beyond ordinary sign-in. It supports use cases where an organization needs a trusted, portable, privacy-aware proof that can be reused across systems and organizational boundaries.
123. 
124. In simple terms, Entra ID helps users sign in to systems. Verified ID helps users prove something about themselves in a way that another party can verify and trust.
125. 
126. Conclusion
127. 
128. Centralized identity systems place a large amount of trust, data, and control in the hands of a limited number of identity providers.
129. 
130. Decentralized identity systems change that model by giving:
131. • users a wallet
132. • organizations reusable signed credentials
133. • verifiers a better way to check claims without collecting everything themselves
134. 
135. That is why verifiable credentials matter. They provide the foundation for a more secure, portable, and privacy-conscious approach to managing identity information.
136. 
137. They make digital trust:
138. • more portable
139. • more private
140. • easier to reuse across real business scenarios
141. 
142. A follow-up article can build on this foundation and explain the technical details behind decentralized identifiers, verifiable presentations, trust systems, issuance flows, verification flows, and the Microsoft Entra Verified ID architecture in more depth.
143.
```

</details>

2.

6.

8.

13. ## Ecosystem Overview
14. 
15. ![01.EcosystemOverview](./01.EcosystemOverview.png)    
16. 
17. 
18. ### Holder 
19. 
20. A **Holder is a role an entity might perform** by 
21. 
22. - possessing one or more verifiable credentials 
23. - generating verifiable presentations from them. 
24. 
25. Example holders include students, employees, and customers.
26. Typically this would be the Wallet App installed on a person's (=the entity) mobile.
27. A holder is usually, but not always, a subject of the verifiable credentials they hold.
28. Holders store their credentials in **credential repositories** (i.e the Wallet App).
29. Holders will **present** the verifiable credential so that they can be verified 
30. in order to grant them (the subject) some kind of access credentials to some resources.
31. 
32. ### Issuer 
33. 
34. **A issuer a role an entity might perform** by: 
35. 
36. - asserting claims about one or more subjects
37. - creating a verifiable credential from these claims
38. - transmitting the verifiable credential to a holder. 
39. 
40. Example issuers include corporations, non-profit organizations, trade associations, governments, and individuals.
41. 
42. ### Credential Repository
43. 
44. A Credential Repository is a program, such as a storage vault or a **personal verifiable credential wallet**, that 
45. **stores verifiable credentials and protects access to them**. For example, the **Microsoft Authenticator App** is
46. one Credential Repository.
47. 
48. ### Subject
49. 
50. An entity about which claims are made. Example subjects include human beings, animals, and things. 
51. In many cases the holder of a verifiable credential is the subject.
52. 
53. ### Verifier
54. 
55. **A Verifiyer is a role an entity performs** by receiving one or more verifiable credentials, 
56. optionally inside a verifiable presentation, for processing. 
57. Example verifiers include employers, security personnel, and websites.
58. 
59. Other specifications might refer to the Verifier role as the **Relying Party**.
60. 
61. 
62. ### Verifiable Data Registry
63. 
64. A **Verifiable Data Registry is a role a system** might perform by **mediating** 
65. the creation and verification of identifiers, keys, and other relevant data, such as: 
66. 
67. - verifiable credential schemas
68. - revocation registries
69. - issuer of public keys
70. - more
71. 
72. which might be required to use verifiable credentials.
73. 
74. In other words **a Verifiable Data Registry is a role a system is a mediator** among:
75. 
76. - the Holder
77. - the Issure
78. - the Verifier
79. 
80. so that the entities with these roles do not have to interact directly with each other in order to 
81. create, transmit, present use and validate verifiable credentials.
82. 
83. ### Verification
84. 
85. Verificatin is the evaluation of whether a verifiable credential or verifiable presentation is an **authentic** 
86. and a timely statement of the issuer or presenter, respectively. 
87. 
88. This includes checking that: 
89. 
90. - the credential (or presentation) conforms to the specification; 
91. - the proof method is satisfied; 
92. - if present, the status check succeeds. 
93. 
94. Verification of a credential **does not imply evaluation of the truth of claims encoded in the credential**, 
95. it only guarantees that the verifiable credentials are value, not true!
96. 
97. This is why often Verifiable Credentils will be combined with **Identiy Proofing**, that is the **process** 
98. that verify the identity the identity of the subject of the credential. The typical analogy is that of
99. a driving license (the verifiable credential) on which some claims about a subject are printed 
100. (claims on the verifiable credential, the driving licence), in which the officer who issues the driving

1. ### The Evolution of Trust: A Two-Part Guide to Decentralized Identity and Microsoft Entra Verified ID
2. 
3. #### Part 1: Foundations of Decentralized Identity (The Conceptual Shift)
4. 
5. ##### 1\. Introduction: The Identity Paradigm Shift
6. 
7. In the current digital landscape, identity is not something we own; it is something we "lease" from centralized authorities. Whether through social media giants, corporate employers, or government agencies, the Identity Provider (IdP) remains the absolute hub of every interaction, controlling the lifecycle, storage, and visibility of user data. We are now witnessing a profound strategic shift toward a user-centric model:  **Decentralized Identity** . In this paradigm, the individual—the  **Holder** —is at the center of the ecosystem. By leveraging a digital wallet, the holder maintains sovereign control over their credentials, deciding exactly when, with whom, and how much information to disclose. This is not merely a technical preference; it is a necessary evolution as the centralized model reaches a breaking point under the weight of systemic security risks and eroding user trust.
8. 
9. ##### 2\. Critiquing the Centralized Status Quo
10. 
11. Professional organizations must move beyond traditional IdP models to mitigate the "Single Point of Failure" inherent in centralization. When a central provider is breached, the fallout is catastrophic, exposing vast amounts of Personal Identifiable Information (PII) to malicious actors. Furthermore, the administrative complexity of managing disparate federations—where every organization must establish direct, manual trust with every other partner—is becoming a bottleneck for agile business operations.| Feature | Centralized Identity Providers | Decentralized Identity Systems || \------ | \------ | \------ || **Data Ownership** | Controlled and stored by the IdP | Owned and controlled by the Holder || **Point of Failure** | Single hub (High-value target) | Distributed (No central data honeypot) || **Federation Complexity** | High; requires manual, direct integration | Low; decoupled through Open Standards || **Privacy & Security** | IdP tracks all activity; high breach risk | Selective disclosure; minimized data footprint |  
12. **Strategic Analysis:**  The shift to decentralization transforms an organization from a "data hoarder" into a "data verifier." By verifying attributes rather than storing raw PII, organizations drastically reduce their GDPR liability and overall security surface area.
13. 
14. ##### 3\. The Three Pillars of Decentralized Trust: The Trust Triangle
15. 
16. The architecture of decentralized trust is built upon a framework known as the  **Trust Triangle** . This model allows digital relationships to be established without the need for pre-existing, direct technical integrations between the issuer and the verifier.
17. 
18. * **The Issuer:**  The entity (e.g., a university or government) that asserts claims about a subject and signs them cryptographically to create a Verifiable Credential (VC).  
19. * **The Holder:**  The individual or entity who receives the VC and stores it securely in a digital wallet (the Credential Repository).  
20. * **The Verifier (Relying Party):**  The entity that requires validation of a claim to grant access or services.**The Trust Triangle Architecture:**
21. 
22.           \[ ISSUER \]   
23.          /          \\  
24.   (1) Issues VC    (3) Verifies via Trust System  
25.        /              \\  
26.       /                \\  
27. \[ HOLDER \] \<---(2) Presents VP---\> \[ VERIFIER \]  
28. \--------------------------------------------------  
29.     \[ TRUST SYSTEM / VERIFIABLE DATA REGISTRY \]  
30.       (Anchors Public Keys & DID Documents)
31. 
32. **Strategic Synthesis:**  The "So What" of this architecture lies in the  **decoupling of trust** . Because the Verifier uses the underlying Trust System (the registry of public keys) to validate the Issuer’s signature, trust is no longer tied to a central broker. This enables a mesh-like ecosystem where trust is universal and instantaneous.
33. 
34. ##### 4\. Strategic Benefits: Business Resilience and User Empowerment
35. 
36. Decentralized identity provides a dual benefit: hardening security posture while removing friction from the user experience.**For Businesses (The Identity of Everything):**
37. 
38. * **Mesh Identity Management:**  Decentralization allows for an "Identity of Everything," including people, pets, buildings, and IoT devices. This creates a mesh of trust without the administrative overhead of creating service principals or central accounts for every non-human entity.  
39. * **Data Minimization:**  Organizations can verify a "Proof of Age" without ever seeing or storing a birth date, moving from a position of data liability to strategic verification.  
40. * **Fraud & Infiltration Mitigation:**  By cryptographically proving identity at the source, businesses can thwart sophisticated "Social Engineering" and nation-state actor infiltration.**For Users:**  
41. * **Sovereign Portability:**  Use a single, verified set of credentials across multiple, unrelated services.  
42. * **Selective Disclosure:**  Present only the necessary data points (e.g., proving "Verified Employee" status without revealing salary or home address).**Case Studies in Risk Mitigation:**  
43. * **Employee Onboarding (Enterprise):**  To counter the rise of North Korean actors infiltrating remote positions, a new hire presents a VC from a trusted identity verifier (e.g., CLEAR). This ensures the human behind the screen is legitimate before any corporate access is granted.  
44. * **Account Recovery (Mitigating the 2023 MGM Hack):**  Centralized help desks are prime targets for social engineering. By requiring a "Face Check" verified against an employee VC during password resets, organizations prevent the type of identity-based infiltration that paralyzed MGM Resorts.
45. 
46. #### Part 2: Technical Architecture and Implementation with Microsoft Entra Verified ID
47. 
48. ##### 1\. The Technical Anatomy of a DID: Standardization of Identity
49. 
50. The robustness of decentralized identity is rooted in the  **Decentralized Identifier (DID)** , a portable, W3C-standardized URI. This standardization is critical for business value, as it ensures interoperability across different vendors and platforms.A DID consists of three segments: did:method:specific-identifier (e.g., did:web:contoso.com).**The DID Document (DID Doc):**  Resolving a DID leads to the  **DID Document** , a JSON file containing the technical anchors of trust. Strategically, the DID Doc contains  **no personal claims** . It includes:
51. 
52. * **Verification Methods:**  Public key material used for cryptographic proof.  
53. * **Service Endpoints:**  Locations for communication and interaction.  
54. * **Linked Domains:**  These provide the "Verified" badge within the Microsoft Authenticator app, giving holders confidence in the issuer's legitimacy.
55. 
56. ##### 2\. The Architecture of Trust: ION vs. DID Web
57. 
58. Organizations must strategically choose a trust system based on their requirements for sustainability and brand reputation.
59. 
60. * **DID ION (Sidetree on Bitcoin):**  A Layer 2 protocol that anchors DIDs to the Bitcoin blockchain.  
61. * *Sustainability Focus:*  ION is a "green" technical choice because it batches thousands of identity operations into a single Bitcoin transaction, providing immutability without massive environmental overhead.  
62. * *Use Case:*  Best for purely permissionless, highly immutable systems independent of a web domain.  
63. * **DID Web (Domain-Based Trust):**  Leverages existing DNS and HTTPS infrastructure.  
64. * *Strategic Advantage:*  It piggybacks on an organization's existing web reputation. The did.json is hosted at https://domain.com/.well-known/did.json.  
65. * *Use Case:*  The default choice for most enterprises looking for a cost-effective, low-complexity deployment.
66. 
67. ##### 3\. Implementing Microsoft Entra Verified ID
68. 
69. Microsoft Entra simplifies the Decentralized Public Key Infrastructure (DPKI) for enterprises by providing an issuance and verification service integrated with Azure.**Requirements for Deployment:**
70. 
71. 1. **Azure Subscription & Key Vault:**  The Key Vault is where the organization's  **private keys**  are securely generated and stored for signing VCs.  
72. 2. **Microsoft Authenticator:**  Acts as the sovereign wallet. While other SDKs exist, Authenticator is required for advanced features like Face Check.**Setup Workflows & Architectural Nuances:**  
73. * **Quick Setup:**  Microsoft manages the hosting of the did.json and the Key Vault. However, this results in a "boring" or "ugly" DID string based on the  **Tenant ID** .  
74. * **Advanced Setup:**  Essential for brand identity. It allows for domain-branded DIDs (e.g., did:web:savilltech.net) and requires the organization to host their own did.json file.
75. 
76. ##### 4\. Advanced Security: Face Check and Liveness Detection
77. 
78. As deep fakes and nation-state imposters evolve, simple credential presentation is insufficient for high-assurance scenarios.  **Face Check**  represents the new security frontier.When a verifier requests a Face Check, the Authenticator app uses  **Azure AI Vision**  to perform a live selfie scan. This is compared against the photo claim in the Verified ID (sourced from Entra or a Government ID).**Strategic Proof: Statistical Significance:**  A verifier receives only a "Confidence Score," ensuring zero PII is shared or stored.
79. 
80. * **50% Score:**  Represents a 1 in 100,000 false positive rate.  
81. * **90% Score:**  Represents a  **1 in a billion false positive rate** , providing the high-assurance verification required for privileged access or sensitive help desk operations.
82. 
83. ##### 5\. Future Outlook: Entitlement Management and Licensing
84. 
85. The long-term vision is a "mesh" identity world where the traditional IdP is no longer the bottleneck.**Integration with Entra Entitlement Management:**  Verified ID can be used to gate  **Access Packages**  (collections of apps and permissions). This allows organizations to grant high-privilege access based on a successful Verified ID presentation and Face Check, ensuring only the legitimate, verified individual can access sensitive resources.**Licensing and Economics:**
86. 
87. * **Issuance & Verification:**  Included in the  **Entra ID P1**  license.  
88. * **High-Assurance Gating:**  Gating Access Packages with Verified ID is part of the  **Entra Suite** .  
89. * **Pooling Logic:**  Organizations receive a pool of  **8 verifications per month, per user** , pooled at the tenant level. (e.g., 10,000 users \= 80,000 monthly verifications).**Final Takeaway:**  Decentralized Identity democratizes trust. By rooting identity in mathematics and open standards, we move toward a digital world that is secure by design, privacy-preserving by default, and resilient against the modern landscape of identity-based threats.
90. 
91.
```

</details>

100. (claims on the verifiable credential, the driving licence), in which the officer who issues the driving
101. luicense document verifies the identity of the subject to whom the verifiable credentials are issued,
102. often by oher means of verifiable and authentifiable identification, i.e. passports or identity documents 
103. issues to the subject by other authorities.
104. 
105. ### Claim 
106. 
107. A Claim an assertion made about a subject.
108. 
109. ### Subject 
110. 
111. The subject is a logical object about which claims are made, i.e. a person, vehicle, employee, etc.
112. 
113. ### Credential and Verifiable Credential
114. 
115. **A credential is a set of one or more claims made by an issuer about an entity**. 
116. 
117. Credentials can also include **metadata used to describe properities of the credential**, 
118. .i.e the issuer or the credential creation date and time and expiration date and time, etc.
119. The **metadata may also be signed by the issuer** to cryptographylly prove that the metadata 
120. was issued by the issuer.
121. 
122. A **verifiable credential is a tamper-evident credential** that has authorship that can 
123. be cryptographically verified. Verifiable credentials can be **used to build verifiable presentations**, 
124. which can also be cryptographically verified. 
125. 
126. The claims in a credential can be about different subjects.
127. 
128. ###  Entity 
129. 
130. Is A thing with distinct and independent existence, such as a person, organization, or device that performs one or more roles in the ecosystem.
131. 
132. ---
133. 
134. ###  Decentralized Identifier 
135. 
136. A **DID** a **portable URL-based identifier**, also known as a DID, **associated with an entity**. 
137. 
138. It is a URI resolvable to DID documents and it is composed of three parts: 
139. 
140. - the scheme did 
141. - a method identifier
142. - a unique method-specific identifier specified by the DID method. 
143. 
144. These identifiers are most often used in a verifiable credential and are associated with subjects 
145. such that a verifiable credential itself **can be easily ported from one repository to another without the need to reissue the credential**. 
146. 
147. An example of a DID is string or text value such as `did:example:123456abcdef` whose parts are:
148. 
149. 1. The Scheme: did
150. 2. The DID Method: example
151. 3. The DID Method-Specific Identifier: 123456abcdef
152. 
153. ---
154. 
155. ### Decentralized Identifier Document 
156. 
157. A DID document, is a digital document that is **accessible using a verifiable data registry** 
158. and **contains information related to a specific decentralized identifier**, such as the 
159. associated repository and public key information.
160. 
161. A DID document typically:
162. 
163. - **DOES NOT** contain any claims related to the user
164. - express one or more Verification Methods
165. - contain cryptographic Public Keys
166. - add services relevant to the interactions with the DID subject
167. 
168. The `did:example:123456abcdef` **resolves to a DID Document**.
169. The DID Doc contains information about the DID, for example it contains the DID **controller**.
170. The values in the DID Doc **are required to very the verifiable credentials**.
171. 
172. Users create, own, and control Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs) independently 
173. of any organization or government. These globally unique identifiers are linked 
174. to Decentralized Public Key Infrastructure (DPKI) metadata, which consists of 
175. JSON documents that contain public key material, authentication descriptors, 
176. and service endpoints.
177. 
178. The DID Doc in short is a container for public cryptographical information and metadata.
179. DID Documents will eventually be stored by a Decentralized Public Key Infrastructure (DPKI)
180. layer that is sometimes referred to as the **Trust System**.
181. 
182. > Example 1: a simple DID Document
183. 
184. ```
185. {
186. "@context": [
187.     "https://www.w3.org/ns/did/v1",
188.     "https://w3id.org/security/suites/ed25519-2020/v1"
189. ]
190. "id": "did:example: 123456789abcdefghi",
191. "authentication": [{
192.     // used to authenticate as did:...fghi
193.     "id": "did:example: 123456789abcdefghi#keys-1",
194.     "type": "Ed25519VerificationKey2020",
195.     "controller": "did:example: 123456789abcdefghi",
196.     "publickeyMultibase": "zH3C2AVvLMv6gmMNam3uVAjZpfkcJCwDwnZn6z3wXmqPV"
197. }]
198. }
199. ```
200. 
201. ![02.DecentralizedIdentifierArchitecture](./02.DecentralizedIdentifierArchitecture.png)    
202. 
203. 
204. ### DID controllers 
205. 
206. The controller of a DID is the entity (person, organization,or autonomous software) 
207. that has the capability (as defined by a DID method) to make changes to a DID document. 
208. This capability is typically asserted by the control of a set of cryptographic keys used 
209. by software acting on behalf of the controller, though it might also be asserted via other 
210. mechanisms.
211. 
212. ### DID subjects 
213. 
214. The subject of a DID is, by definition, the entity identified by the DID. 
215. The DID subject might also be the DID controller. 
216. Anything can be the subject of a DID: person, group, organization, thing, or concept.
217. 
218. ### DID Methods
219. 
220. DID methods such as `did:web` and `did:ion` are the nechanisms by which 
221. a particula type of DID and its associated DID Document are: 
222. 
223. - created
224. - resolved
225. - updated
226. - deactivated
227. 
228. ### DID resolvers and DID resolution 
229. 
230. A DID resolver is a system component that takes a DID as input and produces a conforming DID document as output. 
231. This process is called DID resolution. The steps for resolving a specific type of DID are defined by the relevant 
232. DID method specification.
233. 
234. ### Verifiable data registries 
235. 
236. DIDs are typically recorded on an underlying system or network of some kind, in order to be resolvable to DID documents. 
237. Any system that supports recording DIDs and returning data necessary to produce DID documents is called a verifiable data 
238. registry inrespective of the technology used to implement them. 
239. 
240. Examples include: 
241. 
242. - distributed ledgers
243. - decentralized file systems
244. - databases of any kind
245. - peer-to-peer networks
246. - other forms of trusted and generally distributed data storage such as blockchain
247. 
248. ---
249. 
250. # Interoperability with Current Identity Standards
251. 
252. OpenID standard is used for the two parts
253. 
254. 1. Verifiable Credentials Issuance
255. 2. Verifiable Presentations
256. 
257. ## OpenID for Verifiable Credentials Issuance
258. 
259. The OpenID for Verifiable Credentials Issuance is the specification that 
260. defines the API used to issue verifiable credentials. 
261. 
262. Identity Providers (Issuers) implements this specification to issue verifiable 
263. credentials to Holders.
264. 
265. The access to any implementation of the OpenID for Verifiable Credentials is 
266. **authorized using OAuth 2.0**.  This also menas that **any Wallet application** 
267. **will authorize itself to the any API that issues verifiable creadential with OAuth 2.0**.
268. 
269. **Verifiable Credentials** (VC) are very similar to **identity assertions** such as 
270. **ID Tokens in OpenID Connect**, in that VC allow a **Credential Issuer** to assert
271. End-User claims.
272. 
273. ---
274. 
275. ## OpenID for Verifiable Presentations
276. 
277. > Default Specification
278. 
279. The OpenID for Verifiable Credentials Presentations is the specification 
280. that defines the API used to issue Verifiable Presentations. 
281. 
282. **Holders** implement this specification to issue verifiable presentations 
283. to Verifiers. A Verifier implements the issuance of access token to access 
284. an API based on the verifiable credentials in the verifiable presentation.
285. The access token amy also be signed by the subject, if required; this makes
286. it possible to issue self-signed tokens. 
287. 
288. The OpenID for Verifiable Presentations specification mandates that access to the 
289. implementation API is by authorization via OAuth 2. Therefore, Verifiers are the 
290. role that requests access to the Verifiable Presentations (through the API), 
291. therefore this interaction is authorizated via OAuth 2.  
292. 
293. > Latest Specification: There is a newer standard for OpenID for Verifiable Presentations
294. 
295. Verifiers are normally Wallet applications, such as one of the many 
296. Authenticator Apps that may be installed on a user's mobile.
297. In the default OpenID for Verifiable Presentations just presented 
298. the App obtains an access token through the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Flow.
299. However, the new standard specifies how an an access token may be obtained 
300. by using verifiable credentials already stored in the Wallet.
````

</details>

300. by using verifiable credentials already stored in the Wallet. 
301. 
302. > Combination of the New specification for Verifiable Presentations with the Default Specification
303. 
304. The newer specification implementations of the OpenID for Verifiable Presentations can be combined with the **OpenID Connect** default
305. implementation when it is required that **the ID token is signed by the subject**.
306. 
307. > Combination of the New specification for Verifiable Presentations with other Specifications (for self-issued ID tokens)
308. 
309. .
310. 
311. ---
312. 
313. ## 03 OpenID for User Authentication: (SIOP v2 [Self-Issued OpenID Porvider])
314. 
315. ![03.SIOPv2](./03.SIOPv2.png)    
316. 
317. The OpenID for User Authentication is **distinct from the specifications used for the issuance of verifiable credentials and presentetions**
318. described above. This specification extends OpenID Connect with the concept of a Self-Issued OpenID Provider (Self-Issued OP), 
319. an **OP controlled by the End-User**. 
320. 
321. It can be combined with OpenID for Verifiable Presentations specification.
322. 
323. OpenID Connect defines mechanisms by which an end-user can leverage an **OpenID Provider (OP)** 
324. to **release identity information** about the,eselves, such as authentication and claims, to 
325. a **Relying Party (RP) / Verifier**  which can act on that information. 
326. 
327. In this model, the RP trusts assertions made by the self-issued OP, the self-issued OpenID Provider, 
328. and eliminates the need for a thrid-party identity provider such as Google or Microsoft. 
329. 
330. The Self-Issued OP does not itself assert identity information about this End-user. 
331. Instead, the End-user becomes the issuer of identity information. 
332. Using Self-Issued OPs, the End-Users can authenticate themselves with Self-Issued ID Tokens 
333. signed with keys under the End-user's control and present self-attested claims directly to the RPs. 
334. **There is no dependency on centralized Identity Providers**.
335. 
336. The flow from the image can be described as follows:
337. 
338. 1. a Relying Party, for example and API implementation that needs to autheticate the user issues an OpenID Provider Request to a Self-Issued OpenID Provider, that is a Identity Provider that implements the Self-Issued OpenID Provider API.
339. 
340. 2. The Self-Issued OpenID Provider collect the information about the user that are in the reuest from the RP, whic is enough to identify the user to the Self-Issued OP Provider, and this then carries out Authentication and Authorization against the Wallet of the user. The wallet application of the user, under the control of the user, releases the necessary authorization information about themselves which are also digitally self-signed, and return it ot the OP provider.
341. 
342. 3. The OP Provider returns a OP Porvider Response, a self-issued ID Token, to the Relying Party. The RP does not verify the validity of the assertion made on the idetified user, it just trust them as they are digitally self signed and can only have come from the Wallet of the user themselves. The user is in control of any information they wish to share with the RP.
343. 
344. ---
345. 
346. ## Use Cases
347. 
348. 1. If you only need to present and issue Verifiable Credentials AND you do not need ID Tokens 
349. 
350. Use OpenID for Verifiable Credentials Presentation to issue Verifiable Credentials and 
351. Verifiable Presentations. 
352. 
353. **This standard cannot be used for user authentication**, which means that cannot be used
354. to produce ID Tokens.
355. 
356. 2. When to use (SIOP v2 [Self-Issued OpenID Porvider])
357. 
358. (SIOP) v2 is used to handle user authentication and issue ID Tokens 
359. to Relying Parties (applications / APIs) usinf the user's Wallet Applications
360. which are implementation of SIOP-v2 compliant OPs.
361. 
362. 3. Combining OpenID for Verifiable Credentials and Presentation with SIOP v2
363. 
364. It is possible to combine the standards described above:
365. 
366. - OpenID for the issuance of Verifiable Credentials and Presentation
367. - SIOP v2 [Self-Issued OpenID Porvider]
368. 
369. In this case Verifiable Credentials and Verifiable Presentation issuance
370. is made conditional to the success of a previous user authentication step.
371. Only when the user is successfully authenticated the Verifiable Credentials 
372. and Verifiable Presentation are issued.
373. 
374. 4. Cryptographically Verifiable Claims
375. 
376. - OpenID for the issueance of Verifiable Credentials and Presentation
377. - SIOP v2 [Self-Issued OpenID Porvider]
378. 
379. Self-Issued OPs (OpenID Providers) [SIOP v2] can also present cryptographically 
380. verifiable claims issued by the third parties trusted by the RPs, when used with 
381. separate specifications such as OpenID for Verifiable Credentials Presentation.
382. 
383. This mechanism allows to enhance the ID Tokens by embedding in them verified claims 
384. about the subject that can be passed to the consuming application/API (Rely Party).
385. The advantage of embedding these signed data directly in the ID Token is that the RP 
386. to which this verified information is presented in the ID Token, **no longer needs** 
387. **to interact with Claims Issuers** and therefore the RP interacts directly and 
388. excluively with the user.
389. 
390. In the traditional flow any information concerning the subject of the ID Token is 
391. obtained by the RP by interacting with the corresponding Identity Provider , tipically 
392. one of the many well-known Identity Providers, such as Google, Microsoft, etc.
393. 
394. Conversely with SIOP v2 paradigm **decentralization** is possible in that the RP truts 
395. the enhanced self-igned information included in the ID Tokens issued by a SIOP v2 OP 
396. which makes the interaction with a centralized identity provider unnecessary and the role
397. of the **Claims Issuers** **is no longer required**.  
398. 
399. This is arguably the main achievements of the SIOP v2 paradigm.
400. 
401. ---
402. 
403. # Decentralized System
404. 
405. ![04.DecentralizedSystemv2](./04.DecentralizedSystem.png)     
406. 
407. - the user, and not a Identity Provider, is at the center of this architecture
408. 
409. - data is stored in a decentralized system, which is any system of storage that implements specific protocols for a decentralized storage system, irrespective of the internal implementation details. Examples of technologies that can be use to achieve this goal are: Blockchain, Azure Ledger, etc.
410. 
411. - there is the concept of W3C Decentralized Identifier
412. 
413. - Universal Resolvers are used to retrieve DID Documents
414. 
415. - User can authenticate using Open ID Connect SIOP Compliant Providers
416. 
417. ## Example of user workflow
418. 
419. 1. The user has a wallet application installed on their mobile phone or device
420. 
421. 2. The user 
422. 
423. ### Is a wallet application also used to create or obtain verifiable credentials or variable presentations?
424. 
425. Yes, a wallet application (or digital wallet) is a central component in the ecosystem of verifiable credentials (VCs) and, in relation to Verifiable Presentations (VPs), is used to:
426. 
427. - obtain VPs
428. - store VPs
429. - create VPs
430. 
431. 1. Obtaining Verifiable Credentials (VCs)
432. 
433. The wallet app acts as a secure container to receive and store digital credentials (e.g., mobile driver’s license, diplomas, employee IDs).
434. 
435. - Obtaining: 
436. 
437. The user obtains a credential from an issuer (like a government or university) which is then sent securely to the user's wallet application.
438. 
439. - Secure Storage: 
440. 
441. The wallet acts as a digital repository to store these credentials,   
442. often protected by biometrics.
443. 
444. 2. Creating Verifiable Presentations (VPs)
445. 
446. When a user needs to prove a claim to a verifier (e.g., proving age over 21), the wallet application is used to generate a Verifiable Presentation (VP).
447. 
448. - Creating: 
449. 
450. The wallet signs the presentation with its private key,   
451. proving the user is the rightful owner of the credentials.
452. 
453. - Selective Disclosure: 
454. 
455. The wallet allows users to pick which credentials to share,  
456. and sometimes allows them to reveal only specific data within  
457. a credential (e.g., sharing "over 18" instead of the full birth date).
458. 
459. 3. Key Functions of a Wallet
460. 
461. - Issuance: 
462. 
463. Users receive credentials directly into the wallet.
464. 
465. - Management: 
466. 
467. Users can view, organize, and check the status of their credentials.
468. 
469. - Presentation: 
470. 
471. Users present credentials via QR codes or direct links to third parties.
472. 
473. Refs:
474. 
475. [JWT VC Presentation Profile - Draft](https://identity.foundation/jwt-vc-presentation-profile/)  
476. 
477. ---
478. 
479. # Microsoft Verified ID
480. 
481. ## Core concepts and facts
482. 
483. Microsoft Entra Verified ID uses the OpenID for Verifiable Credentials (Issuance and Presentation) specifications which describes how an issuance or presentation request, and submission should look like when issuers/verifiers communicate with a wallet (Microsoft Authenticator App).
484. 
485. The W3C standards are used by Microsoft Entra Verified ID to define how Verifiable Credentials should look, including which revocation status list spec they follow, etc, so that verifiers from different vendors can understand and verify a presented Verifiable Credentials (for the future interoperability).
486. 
487. The Self-Issued OpenID Provider (SIOP) is used for the id_token_hint attestation flow for Verifiable Credentials claims. We specify `https://self-issued.me` for the issuer instead of an identity provider. In the future it should be possible to authenticate user before the credential is issued.
488. 
489. Azure Active Directory and Azure Active Directory B2C services (at the moment of creating this video) do not support user authentication with Verifiable Credentials. It should be supported in the nearest future so please check the official documentation.
490. 
491. ---
492. 
493. # Microsoft Verified ID example use case
494. 
495. ![05.MicrosoftVerifiedID.HowItWorks](./05.MicrosoftVerifiedID.HowItWorks.png) 
496. ![06.MicrosoftVerifiedID.HowItWorks](./06.MicrosoftVerifiedID.HowItWorks.png) 
497. 
498. 1. The user is a employee at Woodgrove Inc
499. 2. Woodgrove Inc is the issuer fo verifiable credential to the subject, the employee
500. 3. The employee uses thier wallet application (Microsoft Authenticator App) to request the Verifiable Credentials from Woodgrove Inc
501. 4. Woodgrove Inc authenticates the employee and based on claims known about the subject issues to them a verifiable credential that is signed with the issuer, Woodgrove Inc, private key
502. 5. The issuer Woodgrove Inc, stores the correspoonding public key on a decentralized infrastructure
503. 6. The verifiable credential is stored in the subject's wallet
504. 7. The employee at Woodgrove Inc wants to access some resources, such as Apps or APIs available at a (partner) business Fabrikam Co
505. 8. The employee releases a Verifiable Presentation with some claims to Fabrikam Co, the verifier in this interaction.
506. 9. The verifier, Fabrikam Co in this example, can use the PKI and Decentralized Store System to verify the VP.
507. 10. The verifier retrieves the Public Key of the issuer, together with some other metadata, i.e. the DID Document and can verify that the VP is legittimate in that it is from the claimed issuer Woodgrove Inc.
508. 11. The verifier retrieves the Public Key of the subject, and in the same way can verify the ownership of the VP to the subject.
509. 
510. ---
511. 
512. ## Why is a Verifiable Presentation issued by a Wallet application signed with the public key of the issuer and the private key of the subject?
513. 
514. 
515. A Verifiable Presentation (VP) is a holder-controlled data structure 
516. used to securely share Verifiable Credentials (VCs) with a verifier. 
517. It uses a dual-cryptographic signature structure to prove authenticity 
518. and ownership.
519. 
520. - The Issuer's Public Key: 
521. 
522. Proves the underlying credential itself is legitimate. 
523. 
524. Because the Issuer originally signed the credential with their private key, 
525. the verifier uses the Issuer's public key to confirm the data was actually 
526. issued by the trusted party (e.g., a university or government) and hasn't 
527. been tampered with.
528. 
529. - The Subject's Private Key: 
530. 
531. Proves the rightful ownership of the credentials. 
532. 
533. The Wallet application (the holder) signs the entire presentation with 
534. the subject's private key. This allows the verifier to cryptographically 
535. confirm that the person presenting the credentials is the exact same 
536. individual they were originally issued to.
537. 
538. ---
539. 
540. ## The Trust System
541. 
542. DID Documents as container for public cryptographical information and metadata 
543. are stored by a Decentralized Public Key Infrastructure (DPKI) layer that is 
544. sometimes referred to as the **Trust System**.
545. 
546. More precisely, the term **Trust System** does not only refers to the decentrilized
547. storage but also to the infrastructure that tohgether with the decentrilized store
548. enable enitites, such as issuers and verifiers to issue and verify verifiable credentials,
549. respectively, **without direct interaction with each other**.
550.
```

<details>

````
550. 
551. Microsoft, currently, support the following Trust Systems:
552. 
553. ---
554. 
555. 1. DID:ION (Identity Overlay Network) with Blockchain usage.
556. 
557. [DID:ION - GitHub](https://github.com/decentralized-identity/ion)    
558. [Microsoft Security Community Blog ION – We Have Liftoff!](https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/blog/microsoft-security-blog/ion-%E2%80%93-we-have-liftoff/1441555)  
559. 
560. ION is a public, permissionless, Decentralized Identifier (DID) network that implements 
561. the blockchain-agnostic Sidetree protocol on top of Bitcoin (as a 'Layer 2' overlay) to 
562. support DIDs/DPKI (Decentralized Public Key Infrastructure) at scale.
563. 
564. ## What is Microsoft DID:ION ?
565. 
566. ION (Identity Overlay Network) ION is a Layer 2 open, permissionless network based \
567. on the purely deterministic Sidetree protocol. It allows individuals and organizations 
568. to create, own, and control their digital identities securely, independently of any 
569. central authority or corporation.
570. 
571. With ION it is possible to easily anchor the DID on the Bitcoin blockchain.
572. 
573. ###  Here are the core components and features of the ION network:
574. 
575. - No Special Tokens or Validators:  
576. 
577. Unlike many crypto networks, ION relies only on the linear block chronology 
578. of the Bitcoin blockchain for consensus. It does not have its own utility 
579. token or a closed group of trusted validators.
580. 
581. - High Scalability: 
582. 
583. ION operates on Layer 2 using the deterministic Sidetree protocol. 
584. It solves the scalability limits of base blockchains, processing tens of 
585. thousands of identity operations per second.
586. 
587. - True Ownership: 
588. 
589. Because the network is decentralized, users retain absolute ownership of 
590. their digital identity. If a centralized service or account is deleted, 
591. the underlying identity and verifiable credentials remain securely in the 
592. user's control.
593. 
594. - Open Source: 
595. 
596. The code is open-source and maintained within the Decentralized Identity Foundation 
597. (DIF) and is not controlled by Microsoft.
598. 
599. [ION Explorer: a tool to retrive DID Document from its DID](https://identity.foundation/ion/explorer/)
600. 
601. ---
602. 
603. 2. DID:Web is a permission based model that allows trust using a web domain's existing reputation. No blockchain is used here.
604. 
605. [Register your decentralized ID for did:web](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/verified-id/how-to-register-didwebsite)  
606. 
607. ## What is Microsoft DID:Web ?
608. 
609. A DID (Decentralized Identifier) is a secure, user-owned digital identifier that allows individuals 
610. and organizations to control their own identity without relying on centralized third-party providers.
611. It provides a permission based model that allows trust using a web domain's existing reputation.
612. 
613. `did:web` is a specific, permission-based DID method supported by Microsoft. 
614. 
615. It utilizes an organization's existing website domain to establish trust and issue digital identities.
616. 
617. How did:web Works in Microsoft Entra Verified IDSelf-Owned Identity: Instead of relying on a blockchain, Microsoft uses the did:web method to tie an identity to a domain. For example, did:web:microsoft.com.The did.json File: Microsoft Entra generates a did.json document that contains your organization's public keys and service endpoints. This file must be hosted at a specific location on your web server: https://[your-domain]/.well-known/did.json.Verification: When your organization issues a Verifiable Credential, digital wallets (like the Microsoft Authenticator app) resolve the DID by checking your web server. If the keys match, the wallet displays a verified badge, proving the credential's authenticity.Key BenefitsNo Blockchain Required: It uses standard HTTPS and DNS infrastructure, making it much easier and cheaper for companies to adopt.Leverages Existing Reputation: It piggybacks on the trust and reputation your website domain already has.Tamper-Proof: It creates a Decentralized Public Key Infrastructure (DPKI), meaning credentials are cryptographically signed and cannot be faked or altered.
618. 
619. ---
620. 
621. ## DID User Agent/Wallet
622. 
623. - Microsoft Entra Verified ID uses Microsoft Authenticator as Wallet.
624. 
625. - The Authenticator 
626. 
627.     - creates DIDs
628.     - facilitates issuance and presentation requests for verifiable credentials 
629.     - manages the backup of the DID's seed through an encrypted wallet file.
630. 
631. ---
632. 
633. ## Microsoft Resolver
634. 
635. The Microsoft Resolver is used to interact with Decentralized Systems v ia a 
636. standard API.
637. 
638. It is an API that looks up and resolves DIDs using the `did:web` or the `did:ion` 
639. methods and returns the DID Document Object (DDO). 
640. 
641. The DDO includes DPKI metadata associated with the DID such as public keys and service endpoints.
642. 
643. [GitHub: Decentralized Identity Foundation](https://github.com/decentralized-identity)
644. [Web DID Resolver](https://github.com/decentralized-identity/web-did-resolver/blob/master/README.md)

646. # How to resolve DID Documents from their DID Identifier
647. 
648. ---
649. 
650. ## 1. DID Discovery API
651. 
652. [DID Discovery](https://didproject.azurewebsites.net/docs/discovery.html)    
653. 
654. You can use the discovery API to fetch the DID document associated with a DID. 
655. This step will be necessary any time you wish to interact with a DID, including 
656. during authentication. 
657. 
658. This is a developer node and API endpoint operated by Microsoft to resolve and fetch 
659. Decentralized Identifier (DID) documents. It is used for handling decentralized identity 
660. protocols, primarily related to the ION (Identity Overlay Network) and Microsoft Entra Verified.
661. 
662. 
663. To discover a DID, you can send an HTTP request:
664. 
665. ```
666. GET /1.0/identifiers/did:ion-test:EiDDNR0RyVI4rtKFeI8GpaSougQ36mr1ZJb8u6vTZOW6Vw HTTP/1.1
667. Host: beta.discover.did.microsoft.com
668. Accept: application/json
669. ```
670. 
671. ---
672. 
673. ## 2. ION Explorer
674. 
675. [ION Explorer: a tool to retrive DID Document from its DID](https://identity.foundation/ion/explorer/)  
676. 
677. There is however, the tool called `ION Explorer` based on the ION Reolver
678. that can be used in the browser to resolve DID Documents from their DID 
679. Identifier.
680.

693. ```

695. 
696. ### Key Features and UsesDID Resolution: 
697.

699. associated DID document in JSON format. This document contains the public keys and service
700. endpoints required to verify identities and authenticate users.
701. 
702. ### Developer Integration: 
703.

705. secure, verifiable credentials.

745. # Verifiable Credentials Use Cases
746. 
747. ## Enterprise Scenarios
748. 
749. 1. Onboarding and credential issuance
750. 
751. Enterpise have the following general onboarding needs:
752. 
753. - onboard employees
754. - onboard partners
755. - onboard customers
756. 
757. Usually a dedicated Enterprise department, i.e.HR, is in charge of 
758. enrolling the subject and verify their identity through legal documents, 
759. i.e. their government IDs, together with outher legal source of claims 
760. such as their degrees or proof of previous employment, etc. 
761. 
762. Once the identity and claims of the subject have been verified the enterprise
763. wants to issue to the subject some ID in a digital form so that, for example,
764. the subject may be authorized to access some corporate resources that are 
765. relevant to them.
766. 
767. If the issued digital IDs adhere to an open standard, such as Veriable Credentials,
768. then a the mechanisms of issuance, verification and access are going to be uniform.
769. This is the goal of inter-operability that Veriable Credentials achieve.
770.

774. to minimize its complexity and cost through self-servicing. 
775. 
776. Self-servicing reduces support calls to the Help-Desk.
777. 
778. If some verifiable credential remain securely stored on the subject's autheticator
779. app, these can be used to recover their account without Help-Desk intervention.

836. 
837. An Access Package in Microsoft Entra ID Governance is a bundle of resources 
838. such as: 
839. 
840. - applications
841. - group memberships
842. - Microsoft Teams
843. - SharePoint sites
844. 
845. A typical user, according to their roles in the organization, needs some or all of 
846. these resources to perform a specific task as part of their roles.
847. 
848. It automates and secures identity management by combining these resources with request policies, approval workflows, and automatic expiration.
849. 
850. ## Key Components of an Access Package
851. 
852. - Resource Bundling: 
853. 
854. Packages allow administrators to group various IT assets into a single requestable unit. 
855. 
856. Resources like: 
857. 
858.     - Salesforce
859.     - Microsoft 365 Groups
860.     -custom SaaS apps
861. 
862. - Catalogs: 
863. 
864. Access packages must live inside a Catalog, which acts as a secure container 
865. used to delegate administrative rights to specific department or application 
866. owners.
867. 
868. - Policies: 
869. 
870. A package requires one or more policies to dictate who can request the package 
871. (including external guest users), who must approve it, and how long the access 
872. lasts.
873. 
874. ### Why Organizations Use Access Packages
875. 
876. Access packages shift organizations away from manual, ad-hoc access assignments. 
877. 
878. They are highly utilized for:
879. 
880. - Streamlining Onboarding: 
881. 
882. New hires can log into the `My Access portal` and request exactly the set of
883. tools they need for their specific job function.
884. 
885. - Lifecycle Management: 
886. 
887. Because access packages have built-in expiration dates, users do not retain
888. permissions after a project ends. 
889. 
890. - Recurring Access Reviews 
891. 
892. ensure that if access is no longer needed, it is systematically revoked.
893. 
894. - External Collaboration: 
895. 
896. Vendors and contractors can be granted temporary, policy-governed access 
897. to your directory. When their assignment expires, their guest account 
898. is automatically disabled and cleaned up.

Contents of https://www.neteye-blog.com/blog/2026/06/19/decentralized-identity-systems-concepts/:
19\. 06. 2026[Davide Spano](https://www.neteye-blog.com/blog/author/davide-spano/)[Entra](https://www.neteye-blog.com/blog/category/microsoft/entra/), [Microsoft](https://www.neteye-blog.com/blog/category/microsoft/)

# Decentralized Identity Systems Concepts

![](https://www.neteye-blog.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/202606-BLOG-Verified-ID-01.jpg)

Today, most digital identity systems are built around a Central Identity Provider. That provider signs users in, stores key identity data, and often sits in the middle of every trust relationship between people, applications, and organizations.

This model works, but it also creates several growing problems:

1.  Extensive personal data is collected and retained by identity providers (IdPs)
2.  Many organizations need custom trust relationships with each IdP through federation or other explicit trust arrangements
3.  The centralized architecture of each IdP can become a single point of failure

This article explains:

1.  Why decentralized identity is a compelling alternative to the current model
2.  How verifiable credentials improve the way identity can be shared and verified
3.  Why Microsoft Entra Verified ID is an important example of this new approach

### The Problems with the Current Model

In the traditional model, identity is not something people truly own. A user signs in through an identity provider, and that provider controls the credential lifecycle, the sign-in process, and often the way identity data is shared with applications and partner organizations.

This creates four practical problems:

*   **Too much concentration of trust.** When one identity provider or its surrounding infrastructure fails, many connected services can be affected at the same time.
*   **Too much stored data.** Central systems often collect and retain more personal data than a verifier needs for a specific decision.
*   **Too much integration work.** Organizations often need federation, guest accounts, or other explicit trust arrangements to work across company boundaries.
*   **Too little user control.** Users usually have little visibility into who can access their data and how often it is reused.

A good way to summarize the issue is this:

The current model is excellent at account-based access, but not as good at providing portable, privacy-friendly proof. It can say, “this person can sign in here,” but it cannot say, “this person can prove one fact, and nothing more.”

![](https://www.neteye-blog.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/202606-BLOG-Verified-ID-02.jpg)

### What Decentralized Identity Changes

Decentralized identity changes the center of the system. Instead of a central identity provider being involved every time, the user can hold digitally signed credentials in a wallet app on their own device.

These credentials are called verifiable credentials.

An issuer, such as an employer, university, or government office, signs claims about a person or organization, usually after verifying them through official processes and, in some cases, in-person checks. The holder stores that credential in a wallet, and a verifier can later check that the information is authentic and still valid through a trusted distributed framework, without needing to contact the issuer directly every time.

This model is often described using three roles:

*   **Issuer** – the organization that creates and signs the credential
*   **Holder** – the person or entity that stores it in a wallet and chooses when to present it
*   **Verifier** – the organization that checks the credential before granting access, trust, or a service

That shift is important because it turns identity from a stream of repeated account lookups into a system of reusable, trusted proofs.

### A Simple Real-world Analogy

A driving license is a useful analogy. A government authority first checks a person’s identity and eligibility, then issues a document that states certain claims, such as the right to drive a certain class of vehicle, and also age, registered address and other relevant personal and professional information about the holder.

Later, the license holder can show that document to another party, such as a police officer or a car rental company. The third party does not need to call the original authority each time to understand what the document means; they only need to trust the issuer and verify that the document is genuine and belongs to the person presenting it. In general, a police officer will know that the document is legitimate, or may have a way to verify it.

A verifiable credential follows the same broad idea in digital form. The document becomes a cryptographically signed digital credential, the user stores it in a wallet, and the verifier checks the credential instead of collecting and storing large amounts of raw identity data again.

In this model, neither the verifier nor, necessarily, the issuer needs to retain part or even any of the information about the user, instead the information will be packaged in a verifiable digital form signed by the issuer and stored on the holder’s wallet.

### Why This Is Better for Users

The biggest improvement for users is control. A user can keep credentials in a wallet and present them when needed, instead of repeatedly filling in the same forms and sending the same documents to many different services.

This also supports data minimization. In many cases, the verifier doesn’t need a full identity profile. It may only need proof of employment, proof of student status, proof of age, or proof that a person passed an identity check.

That means the user can share less, while still proving enough. This is a better fit for privacy, and it reduces the spread of personal data across many disconnected systems.

### Why This Is Better for Organizations

For organizations, decentralized identity is not just a user-experience improvement. It can also reduce operational friction and lower security risk and liability.

When an organization verifies a signed credential instead of storing every identity detail itself, it can reduce the amount of sensitive data it needs to protect. This can simplify onboarding, partner access, and other trust-based workflows.

There are several clear benefits:

*   **Less repeated identity proofing.** A trusted issuer can verify a person once and issue a reusable credential.
*   **Lower data exposure.** The verifier can ask for the proof it needs instead of collecting a full identity profile.
*   **Cleaner cross-company trust.** Portable credentials can reduce the need to expand trust boundaries through federation for every scenario.
*   **Better fraud resistance.** Credentials are signed and checked cryptographically, which makes tampering harder and verification stronger.
*   **Lower risk and liability.** Credentials, once issued and verified, do not necessarily need to be stored by the organizations that issue or consume them. This reduces the amount of sensitive data they must retain and can make them less attractive targets for malicious actors.

This is why decentralized identity is often best understood as an addition to existing identity systems, rather than always as a full replacement. Centralized identity still works well inside a company’s own trust boundary, while verifiable credentials are especially useful when trust needs to move across boundaries.

Technologies and implementations such as Microsoft Entra Verified ID make this transition possible for organizations that want to adopt this model.

### The Role of the Wallet

The wallet is central to the user experience. In Microsoft’s documentation, Microsoft Authenticator is the wallet app that can:

*   Create decentralized identifiers
*   Receive issuance requests
*   Store credentials
*   Respond to presentation requests

That matters because the wallet is where the user’s control becomes real. The user can review a request, consent to share a credential, and keep a record of where that credential was presented
